content.problemStatement:
CodeChef admins went on shopping at a shopping mall.

There are NNN shops in the mall where the ithi^{th}ith shop has a capacity of AiA_iAi​ people. In other words, at any point in time, there can be **at most** AiA_iAi​ number of people in the ithi^{th}ith shop.

There are XXX admins. Each admin wants to visit each of the NNN shops **exactly once**. It is known that an admin takes exactly **one** hour for shopping at any particular shop. Find the **minimum** time (in hours) in which all the admins can complete their shopping.

**Note:**


An admin can visit the shops in any order.
It is possible that at some point in time, an admin sits idle and does not visit any shop while others are shopping.

Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and XXX - the number of shops and the number of admins.
The second line of each test case contains NNN integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ - the capacity of the shops.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line the **minimum** time (in hours) in which all the admins can complete their shopping.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow.
For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and X — the number of shops and the number of admins.
- The second line contains N integers A1, A2, …, AN — the capacity of each shop.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible number of hours required for all admins to finish shopping.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Minimum time required to complete the shopping is two hours. A possible way to complete shopping in 222 hours is :

1st1^{st}1st hour: All 333 admins visit shop 111. This is possible as the capacity of the shop is 3≥33 \geq 33≥3.
2nd2^{nd}2nd hour: All 333 admins visit shop 222. This is possible as the capacity of the shop is 3≥33 \geq 33≥3.

Test case 222: Minimum time required to complete the shopping is 444 hours. A possible way to complete shopping in 444 hours is :

1st1^{st}1st hour: Admin 111 visits shop 111 and admin 222 visits shop 444.
2nd2^{nd}2nd hour: Admin 111 visits shop 222 and admin 222 visits shop 333.
3rd3^{rd}3rd hour: Admin 111 visits shop 333 and admin 222 visits shop 222.
4th4^{th}4th hour: Admin 111 visits shop 444 and admin 222 visits shop 111.

Test case 333: Minimum time required to complete the shopping is 333 hours. A possible way to complete shopping in 333 hours is :

1st1^{st}1st hour: Admins 111 and 222 visits shop 111. All other admins sit idle.
2nd2^{nd}2nd hour: Admins 333 and 444 visits shop 111. All other admins sit idle.
3rd3^{rd}3rd hour: Admins 555 and 666 visits shop 111. All other admins sit idle.
Note that, since the capacity of shop is 222, maximum 222 admins visit the shop at once.

solution.approach:
For each test case we need the smallest integer H such that a feasible schedule exists.

*Every admin* must visit N shops, one per hour → at least N hours are necessary.
*Shop i* can accommodate at most Ai admins per hour, therefore it needs at least ceil(X / Ai) hours to serve all X admins.
In total there are X·N visits. In one hour the whole mall can serve at most sum(Ai) admins, so at least ceil((X·N) / sum(Ai)) hours are required.
These three lower bounds are also sufficient: when H is at least the maximum of the three, a schedule can be built by repeatedly assigning admins to any shop that still has remaining capacity in the current hour (a flow‑based argument shows feasibility). Hence the answer is:

    answer = max( N , max_i ceilDiv(X, Ai) , ceilDiv(X·N, sumA) )
where ceilDiv(a,b) = (a + b - 1) / b.
The algorithm scans the capacities once, computes sumA, the maximum of the per‑shop ceiling values, and the total‑work ceiling. All operations are O(N) per test case.

**Correctness proof sketch**
1. *Lower bounds* – explained above; any feasible schedule must respect each of them.
2. *Achievability* – Let H be the maximum of the three bounds. For every shop i we have H·Ai ≥ X, so we can allocate X “slots” of shop i across the H hours. Also H·N ≥ X·N, therefore the total number of admin‑hour slots (H·N) is at least the total number of required visits. Construct a bipartite graph with admins on the left (degree N) and shops on the right (degree X). Because each shop i has H·Ai ≥ X incident “capacity edges”, we can split each shop vertex into Ai parallel copies, each copy having capacity H. The resulting graph is regular with degree ≤ H on both sides, and by König’s line‑coloring theorem it can be edge‑colored with H colors, giving a schedule that respects all constraints.
Thus the computed maximum is both necessary and sufficient.

**Complexity analysis**
- Time: O(N) per test case, overall O( Σ N ) ≤ O(2·10^5).
- Memory: O(1) extra besides input storage.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (overall O( Σ N )).

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def ceil_div(a, b):
    return (a + b - 1) // b

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        X = int(next(it))
        sum_a = 0
        max_ceil = 0
        for _ in range(N):
            a = int(next(it))
            sum_a += a
            cur = ceil_div(X, a)
            if cur > max_ceil:
                max_ceil = cur
        total_ceil = ceil_div(X * N, sum_a)
        ans = max(N, max_ceil, total_ceil)
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
